title: From Self To Others
study: A Study In The Life Of Moses
scriptureForWeek: VARIOUS PASSAGES IN EXODUS Make sure you focus on the verses for each day, as these important events are taken from several readings of scripture across several chapters. These are chosen to give a few important and closing lessons on Moses’ leadership failure and challenges in the wilderness wandering.
weekTheme: Boys are Selfish, Men Love Believe it or not, this week is the end of shift 2: Self to Others. J. R. R. Tolkien who wrote The Hobbit and Lord of the Rings wrote: “Living by faith includes the call to something greater than cowardly self- preservation.” That’s what you have been doing: learning to move beyond the cowardly self-preservation that is a disease in the hearts of so many young men. This week, you will finish studying one of the greatest men who has ever lived (Moses) and reflect on some powerful lessons about breaking free from the immaturity of selfishness. But this week will come with some warnings. This week, you will see the real-life challenges of sustaining love for the long haul. Moses will get frustrated, and the grumbling of the people will cause him to disobey God and face massive consequences. You will also learn about the power of a godly legacy and how you can help others experience God’s blessing by making wise choices. And you will see how Moses’ life of selfless love will leave a legacy that will last thousands of years and go on to inspire billions.

days.title: Love Is Worth It
days.scripture: HEBREWS 11:24-27 It was by faith that Moses, when he grew up, refused to be called the son of Pharaoh’s daughter. He chose to share the oppression of God’s people instead of enjoying the fleeting pleasures of sin. He thought it was better to suffer for the sake of Christ than to own the treasures of Egypt, for he was looking ahead to his great reward. It was by faith that Moses left the land of Egypt, not fearing the king’s anger. He kept right on going because he kept his eyes on the one who is invisible.
days.reflection: After spending the last 9 weeks looking at the life of Moses and his sacrificial love, something might be rising in your heart. Something you don’t want to say out loud. You may be thinking, “living for others isn’t worth it.” Moses went through a ton of drama to lead the Israelites to freedom, and when he did, they didn’t even seem thankful. They changed their minds all the time, complained non- stop, criticized him, and were really ungrateful. They talked about going back to Egypt and rebelled against his leadership. Moses’ life would have been so much easier if he had just stayed in the palace, let God worry about the Israelites, and became Pharaoh. He might have even been able to set them free if he became the king. But look at what the verse from today's reading says. “He chose to share the oppression of God’s people instead of enjoying the fleeting pleasures of sin.” He chose the people over pleasure. He chose love over selfishness. It can be really tempting as a young guy to just shut people out and only worry about yourself. Worry about friends, girls, video games, sports, a few classes and that’s it. You may even have been hurt by trying to help other people or been rejected when you were trying to be kind. But if we don’t take the risk of love, our hearts will die. Remember St Augustine’s idea from a few weeks back? Sin is called incurvatus, or love turned in on itself. To really grow up, you have to move outside yourself and not collapse inward. C.S. Lewis, who wrote The Chronicles of Narnia, says this really well in his book The Four Loves. “There is no safe investment. To love at all is to be vulnerable. Love anything, and your heart will certainly be wrung and possibly be broken. If you want to make sure of keeping it intact, you must give your heart to no one, not even to an animal. Wrap it carefully round with hobbies and little luxuries; avoid all entanglements; lock it up safe in the casket or coffin of your selfishness. But in that casket – safe, dark, motionless, airless – it will change. It will not be broken; it will become unbreakable, impenetrable, irredeemable. The alternative to tragedy, or at least to the risk of tragedy, is damnation. The only place outside Heaven where you can be perfectly safe from all the dangers and perturbations of love is Hell.” All great men that we admire and remember are men who live for others. Men who sacrifice for something greater than themselves. Men who put others before themselves. The greatest of these was Jesus. He gave his life to pay the guilt of our sin when he was innocent. He was loyal to his friends when they walked away. And he wants to train you to become like him. To be a man who can handle whatever comes your way. To be a man who can forgive, love, serve, and hold your own no matter what relational challenges you face. Love is worth it. Jesus loved you and thought you were worth it too.
questions: In the New Testament, Jesus and Moses are contrasted as being similar. Can you think of a few ways Jesus and Moses modeled “self to others”?
What is one of your favorite movie scenes where the hero sacrifices himself for the sake of others? Why is this so inspiring?
Ask the person you are doing this study with to share how they really learned to grasp the idea of the shift from self to others. What difference has it made in their life?

thoughts.verse: Most important of all, continue to show deep love for each other, for love covers a multitude of sins.
prompts: Is there something that stands out to you in it?
Is there a question you have about any of the words and what they mean?
Do you think you could explain what it means to someone else if they asked?

days.prayer: Take a moment to ask God to give you a heart of sacrificial love for others. Ask him to give you joyful sacrifice, and not just dutiful sacrifice. Thank him for the example of godly men from history, and ask him to stir that same vision in you.

days.title: The Loss Of Lashing Out
days.scripture: NUMBERS 20:1-13 In the first month of the year, the whole community of Israel arrived in the wilderness of Zin and camped at Kadesh. While they were there, Miriam died and was buried. There was no water for the people to drink at that place, so they rebelled against Moses and Aaron. The people blamed Moses and said, “If only we had died in the LORD’s presence with our brothers! Why have you brought the congregation of the LORD’s people into this wilderness to die, along with all our livestock? Why did you make us leave Egypt and bring us here to this terrible place? This land has no grain, no figs, no grapes, no pomegranates, and no water to drink!” Moses and Aaron turned away from the people and went to the entrance of the Tabernacle, where they fell face down on the ground. Then the glorious presence of the LORD appeared to them, and the LORD said to Moses, “You and Aaron must take the staff and assemble the entire community. As the people watch, speak to the rock over there, and it will pour out its water. You will provide enough water from the rock to satisfy the whole community and their livestock.” So Moses did as he was told. He took the staff from the place where it was kept before the LORD. Then he and Aaron summoned the people to come and gather at the rock. “Listen, you rebels!” he shouted. “Must we bring you water from this rock?” Then Moses raised his hand and struck the rock twice with the staff, and water gushed out. So the entire community and their livestock drank their fill. But the LORD said to Moses and Aaron, “Because you did not trust me enough to demonstrate my holiness to the people of Israel, you will not lead them into the land I am giving them!” This place was known as the waters of Meribah (which means “arguing”) because there the people of Israel argued with the LORD, and there he demonstrated his holiness among them.
days.reflection: Here we go again. The children of Israel run out of water and freak out. They dredge up the same old complaints they have been saying for 40 years. “We are going to die. You should have left us in Egypt. There is no water. There is no food. We want figs and grapes and pomegranates!” You would think that after all God has done for them, they would have learned to trust him by now. But they haven’t. Moses goes back to God with a familiar request, and God gives him instructions. Speak to the rock and water will flow out. Seems simple enough. But after all this time, Moses has had enough. Instead of speaking to the rock, he strikes the rock. Look at his tone here. “Listen, you rebels!” he shouted. “Must we bring you water from this rock?” Water flows and the people are fine, but tragically Moses isn’t. In his frustration, he has disobeyed God. He has attributed the miracle to himself, yelling “Must we” instead of “Must God.” He has lashed out in anger, calling the people “rebels.” He has failed to trust God and did things his own way. What a tragedy. One public moment of anger will cost him his future. God tells Moses that he will not be allowed into the promised land because of this. He will only be allowed to see it from a distance. Joshua will be the one to take the people in. Public failure has public consequences. Later on, Moses will complain to the Israelites that it was their fault that he missed the promised land. In Deuteronomy 3:26, he says, “But the LORD was angry with me because of you, and he would not listen to me. ‘That’s enough!’ he declared. ‘Speak of it no more.” He is blaming those he is called to serve, and his heart has slowly shifted in a tragic way. This is such an important lesson for you to carry in your heart as you deal with people. Lashing out in anger may feel natural, but it often leads to disaster. Even though it’s a normal human reaction, it can have devastating consequences. We can really hurt the people we lash out at. We can say terrible things we can't take back. We can do damage to relationships that are hard to repair. We can be remembered for a moment of weakness, rather than a legacy of love. It’s so important to really process and think through how you treat others when they frustrate you, drive you crazy, or make you mad. When you get angry, take a moment to calm your heart. Take a few breaths, and think about the kind of guy you want to be. Respond out of your values, not just emotions. You may not be able to choose how people treat you, but you can choose how to respond to them. There is a lot at stake when you live for others. Remember Jesus on the cross with such a calm spirit? Not striking back, not screaming at the soldiers. Resolute in love, he succeeded where Moses failed. Ask God to give you grace to respond like Jesus, not lash out in anger. There is a lot at stake in your life ahead.
questions: When you get angry, do you tend to go passive or lash out? Do you internalize or externalize your anger?
What are some ways to not lash out when you get angry? Take a moment to brainstorm a few ideas.

Ask the person you are doing this study with to share a time they lashed out at someone in anger and regretted it. What happened, what did they say, what damage did they do? What consequences came from it, and how did they work to repair things?

thoughts.verse: Fools vent their anger, but the wise quietly hold it back.
prompts: Is there something that stands out to you in it?
Is there a question you have about any of the words and what they mean?
Do you think you could explain what it means to someone else if they asked?

days.prayer: Take a moment to bring any anger or frustration you have towards others before God. Ask him to give you patience, self-control, and love for those driving you crazy.

days.title: Helping Others Choose Well
days.scripture: DEUTERONOMY 30:11-20 “This command I am giving you today is not too difficult for you, and it is not beyond your reach. It is not kept in heaven, so distant that you must ask, ‘Who will go up to heaven and bring it down so we can hear it and obey?’ It is not kept beyond the sea, so far away that you must ask, ‘Who will cross the sea to bring it to us so we can hear it and obey?’ No, the message is very close at hand; it is on your lips and in your heart so that you can obey it. “Now listen! Today I am giving you a choice between life and death, between prosperity and disaster. For I command you this day to love the LORD your God and to keep his commands, decrees, and regulations by walking in his ways. If you do this, you will live and multiply, and the LORD your God will bless you and the land you are about to enter and occupy. “But if your heart turns away and you refuse to listen, and if you are drawn away to serve and worship other gods, then I warn you now that you will certainly be destroyed. You will not live a long, good life in the land you are crossing the Jordan to occupy. “Today I have given you the choice between life and death, between blessings and curses. Now I call on heaven and earth to witness the choice you make. Oh, that you would choose life, so that you and your descendants might live! You can make this choice by loving the LORD your God, obeying him, and committing yourself firmly to him. This is the key to your life. And if you love and obey the LORD, you will live long in the land the LORD swore to give your ancestors Abraham, Isaac, and Jacob.”
days.reflection: Our whole life is defined by our choices; therefore, a man must learn to choose well. If you love others and want them to thrive, you will care about the choices they make. You will do everything in your power to help them make choices that honor God and lead to blessing. For so many young guys, the choices they make are defined by peer pressure. This can lead to making incredibly poor decisions. They choose to pick on others to fit in, try drugs to look cool, or go too far physically with girls, all so others think they are cool. But poor decisions can have disastrous consequences for our lives. Though they seem fun at the moment, the long-term damage can be unreal. Be careful what choices you make, young man. On the flip side, good choices can lead to incredible blessings. Choosing well can help you make lifelong friends, date the right woman, build wealth, and have a deep and meaningful relationship with God. You can live the kind of life God can trust and bless. Moses calls godly choices “the key to your life.” So what makes a good choice according to the Bible? How do you help others choose based on God's vision and not the world’s? 1-Don’t give in to the excuse that godly choices are too hard. Moses says, “The message is very close at hand; it is on your lips and in your heart so that you can obey it.” The Bible is clear about what God wants, what honors him, and what leads to blessing. It’s not fuzzy, confusing, or irrelevant. It is challenging, but it is clear. Remind people God has made his will known and that choosing his will is worth it. 2-Help people think about the long-term consequences of their decisions, not the pleasure of the moment. Again, Moses warns, “But if your heart turns away and you refuse to listen, and if you are drawn away to serve and worship other gods, then I warn you now that you will certainly be destroyed. You will not live a long, good life in the land you are crossing the Jordan to occupy." When we are tempted, sin minimizes the consequences, and maximizes the pleasure. But as soon as the sin is over, it maximizes the consequences and minimizes the pleasure. Don’t fall into this trap. Think big picture, think long term, think beyond the temptation. 3-Remind people of the cool stuff that comes from good choices. “If you do this, you will live and multiply, and the LORD your God will bless you and the land you are about to enter and occupy.” There are rewards for doing what is right. When you have integrity of heart and live without shame, you inspire others as to what a proper man looks like, and you gain favor from God and other people because you show yourself to be trustworthy and wise. Your whole life will come down to the choices you make. Moses urged the Israelites to choose so that they may live. He urges you to make these choices too and to provide positive peer pressure so your friends can as well. Be careful what choices you make, young man.
questions: Do you experience peer pressure to make bad choices? If so, where does it come from and how do you feel it affects you?

Can you think of a poor choice you have made this year? What influenced your thinking in the decision? Can you think of a good choice you made this year? What influenced your thinking in that? What was different in your thinking between the two?
Ask the person you are doing this study with to share a poor decision they have made and the consequences of it, and one of the best decisions they have made and the blessing they got from it.

thoughts.verse: Trust in the LORD with all your heart; do not depend on your own understanding. Seek his will in all you do, and he will show you which path to take.
prompts: Is there something that stands out to you in it?
Is there a question you have about any of the words and what they mean?
Do you think you could explain what it means to someone else if they asked?

days.prayer: Ask God to help you in any area where you are struggling to make wise decisions. Ask him to bless you for any steps of obedience you are taking right now.

days.title: You'Re Up
days.scripture: DEUTERONOMY 31:7-8 Then Moses called for Joshua, and as all Israel watched, he said to him, “Be strong and courageous! For you will lead these people into the land that the LORD swore to their ancestors he would give them. You are the one who will divide it among them as their grants of land. Do not be afraid or discouraged, for the LORD will personally go ahead of you. He will be with you; he will neither fail you nor abandon you.”
days.reflection: Moses is dead. He isn’t going to lead your generation into the things of God. Joshua is dead; he isn’t going to lead this generation either. Abraham, Isaac, Jacob, and Joseph are dead too. David is dead; He isn’t going to show up and kill your giants. The disciples are dead. In fact, everyone in the Bible is dead. But you know who isn’t? You. You are here today, as a young man, with a great life in front of you. But what you do with that life will have massive ramifications. In every generation, God looks to find young men who will follow him with all their hearts, so that he can accomplish his purposes through them. Moses knew his life had come to an end, and it was time to empower the next leader to bring the children of Israel into their inheritance. He had done his part and laid a foundation that others could build on. But now it was time for someone else to step up. And Moses gives these instructions for how to be a godly man in your own generation. Be strong and courageous. Remember the quote from Tolkien at the start of this week? “Living by faith includes the call to something greater than cowardly self- preservation.” It takes courage to love others. Courage to care about something bigger than yourself. Courage to put the needs of others before your own. So take heart, be strong, and step up. The world is aching for selfless leaders today. Don’t get discouraged. As you have learned these last weeks, when you live for others it can beat you down. People can be halfhearted, disloyal, and grumble till you want to scream. But don’t let that hold you back. Get back up, dust your discouragement off, and keep serving. Don’t worry about recognition; worry about being faithful. God sees and will reward even the smallest thing you do in his name. Trust in God’s presence. “the LORD will personally go ahead of you. He will be with you; he will neither fail you nor abandon you.” You know who else isn’t dead? Jesus. He promises to be with you and to help you in every situation you face. So remember you are not on your own trying to be a heroic person. Rely on his power and help, and you will be amazed at what he can do through you. You haven’t studied these last 10 weeks to get a bunch of information dumped in your head. You haven’t studied just so you know the Bible a bit more. You have learned these things so you can live these things. It's your turn to make the shift from self to others. God doesn’t need the people of the Bible to come back to life. He has you, here and now. Don’t be afraid. Don’t be discouraged. God will be with you in all that you do.
questions: Is there anything in your heart that you would love to see God do in your life? If so, would you be willing to share it?
Whose leadership do you resonate more with - Moses or Joshua? Why?

Ask the person you are doing this study with to share a moment when he realized it was his turn to accept reasonability for living for God in his generation. What happened, and how did it impact his life?

thoughts.verse: The eyes of the LORD search the whole earth in order to strengthen those whose hearts are fully committed to him.
prompts: Is there something that stands out to you from it?
Is there a question you have about any of the words and what they mean?
Do you think you could explain what it means to someone else if they asked?

days.prayer: Take a moment to ask God to give you a heart to serve him in this generation. Ask him for courage and encouragement in any areas you are struggling to love others well.

days.title: A Legacy Of Love
days.scripture: DEUTERONOMY 34:1-8 Then Moses went up to Mount Nebo from the plains of Moab and climbed Pisgah Peak, which is across from Jericho. And the LORD showed him the whole land, from Gilead as far as Dan; all the land of Naphtali; the land of Ephraim and Manasseh; all the land of Judah, extending to the Mediterranean Sea; the Negev; the Jordan Valley with Jericho—the city of palms—as far as Zoar. Then the LORD said to Moses, “This is the land I promised on oath to Abraham, Isaac, and Jacob when I said, ‘I will give it to your descendants.’ I have now allowed you to see it with your own eyes, but you will not enter the land.” So Moses, the servant of the LORD, died there in the land of Moab, just as the LORD had said. The LORD buried him in a valley near Beth-peor in Moab, but to this day no one knows the exact place. Moses was 120 years old when he died, yet his eyesight was clear, and he was as strong as ever. The people of Israel mourned for Moses on the plains of Moab for thirty days, until the customary period of mourning was over.
days.reflection: When you really learn to become a man and make the shift from living for yourself to living for others, you need to understand that this is a lifelong quest. It’s really a whole different way to live. It’s not a one-off event, a moment of kindness, or a few caring feelings. It's seeing the whole of your life as an adventure of love that defines all you do. You see, the temptation for guys your age is to do a few things for others while keeping a selfish heart. It's doing acts of kindness without becoming kind. It's serving here and there, without taking on the nature of a servant. It can feel good to do big things for others that others respect and give you credit for. It can feel good to do good without becoming good. It can be great to celebrate the miracle but not be faithful in the mundane. As it turns out, deliverance is easy; faithfulness is hard. Reflecting on the story of Moses and the Israelites, one author notes, As it turns out, freedom from serving Pharaoh is the easy bit. From beginning to end, it takes only fourteen chapters. Freedom to serve God, on the other hand, takes forty years of wandering and the next four books. (Echoes of Exodus) Love plays the long game. Love learns to be patient over a lifetime. When Moses died, he left behind a remarkable legacy. The Bible contains 66 different books, and Moses is mentioned in 32 of them. He started off living for himself, enjoying the power and privilege of the palace. Then he acted to set people free, but he did it in his own strength. Then he spent 40 years being shaped into a man God could really use. Someone humble and kind. Then he spent 40 years delivering and leading the Israelites as God's appointed leader. And God was pleased with Moses. He spoke to him face to face as with a friend. And at the end of his life, God buried Moses himself. And now you have this vision set before you. To decide in your heart to move away from the selfishness that dominates the lives of most guys today in order to become a young man who learns to live for others from a heart of love. May God grant you power to do this, to have patience with others, and a vision of leaving a really cool legacy like Moses did.
questions: What have you sensed happening in your heart over the past 10 weeks studying the shift from self to others?

Reflect on this quote: “Carve your name on hearts, not tombstones. A legacy is etched into the minds of others and the stories they share about you.” Shannon L. Alder Have you ever thought about the legacy you are going to leave with your life? Are there any things you would like to be known for after you are going?
Ask the person you are doing this study with to share their hopes and vision for their own legacy. Ask them to describe how they want to be remembered and what they hope to leave behind.

thoughts.verse: Choose a good reputation over great riches; being held in high esteem is better than silver or gold.
prompts: Is there something that stands out to you in it?
Is there a question you have about any of the words and what they mean?
Do you think you could explain what it means to someone else if they asked?

days.prayer: Take a moment to ask God to fill your heart with a vision of leaving a legacy of love. Ask him to show you what you can do today to love and serve others.

extras: WEEK 1 0 MANSCHOOL NO MAN SCHOOL This week there is no man school because you are going to be executing DIFFICULTY DAY 2. This one is going to take a little more prep, but it will be deeply formative.
